title: Mofongo
subtitle: Puerto Rican fried plantain mashed with garlic and crackling
excerpt: Fried green plantain pounded in a pilón with garlic, olive oil, and chicharrón into a dense, fragrant mash. Puerto Rico's most distinctive dish: starchy, garlicky, substantial.
sourceNotes: Mofongo is Puerto Rico's most iconic dish, made by frying green plantain until golden, then pounding it in a wooden mortar (pilón) with garlic, olive oil, and chicharrón (pork crackling) into a dense mass. The African root of the dish comes from the West African fufu tradition of pounding starchy root vegetables. Green plantain is not widely available in the UK; use green, unripe cooking bananas as the nearest substitute. The result will be denser than true mofongo but structurally identical. Chicharrón can be approximated with pork scratchings from a UK supermarket; or render small cubes of pork belly skin yourself. Mofongo is traditionally shaped into a dome or served in the mortar. It is eaten as a side, stuffed with shrimp in garlic sauce, or topped with any braised meat.

content.text: Mofongo is not complicated but it requires a mortar, specifically a large wooden pilón, or a heavy stone pestle and mortar that can hold enough fried plantain for one portion. The plantain goes in hot from the oil, the garlic and crackling go in with it, and you pound until the mixture is cohesive but not smooth. There should be texture: some chunks, some crunchy bits of crackling, the garlic absorbed throughout.

The green banana substitute works in the UK; the result is denser and less sweet than true green plantain but the method is identical. Do not use ripe or yellow plantain, the dish needs the starchy firmness of unripe fruit. Pork scratchings from a supermarket are an entirely functional substitute for chicharrón.

items.name: Banana
items.prepNote: green, unripe cooking bananas, not ripe or sweet

items.name: Pork belly
items.prepNote: skin-on, cut into 1 cm cubes and rendered until crisp, or 60 g pork scratchings

items.name: Chicken stock
items.prepNote: warm, for loosening the mofongo if too stiff

content.text: If using fresh pork belly: place the {{pork-belly}} of cubed skin-on pork belly in a dry frying pan over medium heat. Render for 10 to 15 minutes, turning occasionally, until the pieces are crisp and golden and have released their fat. Remove and drain. (Skip this step if using ready-made pork scratchings.)

Score and peel the {{banana}} green bananas. Cut each into rounds about 3 cm thick. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a deep saucepan to 175°C. Fry the rounds in batches for 4 to 5 minutes until golden and cooked through. Remove with a slotted spoon and drain on kitchen paper. Work quickly, the rounds need to go into the mortar while still hot.

Work one portion at a time. Place a quarter of the hot fried rounds in the mortar along with a quarter of the {{garlic}} of minced garlic, a splash of the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil, and a quarter of the crackling pieces. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Pound firmly, not to a smooth paste, but until the mixture coheres into a rough, dense mass with visible bits of crackling throughout. If it is too stiff, add a splash of warm {{stock-chicken}} chicken stock and work it in. Press into a dome shape in a bowl or the mortar and serve immediately. Repeat for the remaining portions.

items.fix: The plantain must be pounded while hot. It hardens quickly as it cools. If it is too dry, add warm chicken stock a splash at a time and pound it in. More olive oil also helps bind the mass.
items.cause: Plantain cooled before pounding; not enough olive oil
items.symptom: Mofongo dry and crumbling rather than cohesive

items.fix: Mofongo needs a generous amount of garlic, more than might seem appropriate. Taste after pounding and add more garlic and salt before shaping.
Maintain 175°C and fry for a full 4 to 5 minutes. Cut rounds no more than 3 cm thick. The inside should be fully soft before the outside is golden.
items.cause: Oil too hot; rounds cut too thick
items.symptom: Plantain rounds not cooking through before the outside browns

items.fix: Render the pork belly cubes until all the fat has come out and the pieces are fully crisped. Pat dry before adding to the mortar, moisture from the frying oil will soften crackling quickly.
items.cause: Pork belly not rendered dry enough; moisture in the pan

content.text: Mofongo is the most distinctly Puerto Rican thing on any Puerto Rican menu. Its lineage runs directly from West African fufu, the technique of pounding starchy root vegetables into a cohesive mass, brought to the Caribbean by enslaved West Africans and adapted to the plantain that grew there. The garlic and olive oil are Spanish. The result is entirely Puerto Rican. Mofongo appears in dozens of forms: plain as a side, stuffed with shrimp in garlic butter, hollowed out and filled with braised beef, or served in a broth as sopa de mofongo. The pilón, the wooden mortar, is a kitchen object of cultural significance; the name itself comes from the Kongo word mfwenge-mfwenge, meaning a mass of mashed food.
